section_title: INTRODUCTION TO TRADE PSYCHOLOGY & EFFECTIVE JOURNALING
content:
Welcome to this teaching specifically dealing with trade psychology and effective journaling. The points of
focus in this module are:
- Major psychological barriers and personal opinions on solutions for overcoming them.
- Effective journaling and how it can assist your development.

section_title: OVERCOMING THE FEAR OF MISSING MOVES (FOMO)
content:
The first major psychological barrier is the fear of missing moves. This is personally believed to be rooted in
not fully understanding what your setup is. Every trader will have their specific setup, which may or may not
include an indicator. Price action alone can be sufficient. If you rely on an indicator for a buy or sell
decision, you need to stick with it and know that's the one you're going to trade. If you have defined that
setup in a written form, i.e., a trading plan, you won't have the same feeling a neophyte or less organized
trader would have. This doesn't guarantee profitability, but it reduces the fear of missing a move. An
ambiguous or untimely pattern that lacks consistency in time and price will lead to hit-and-miss results,
often falsely attributed to the setup when it's just randomness.

section_title: MITIGATING FOMO WITH DEFINED SETUPS AND KILL ZONES
content:
To overcome the fear of missing moves, you must have a well-defined plan and intimately know your setup.
This intimate knowledge will keep you far less anxious because you'll know when they're most likely going
to form. This is why 'kill zones' are used a time element of the trading day. Knowing your setup, the time it
should occur, the days of the week it should form, and whether it's a bullish or bearish model reduces
anxiety about missing a move. Even if a trade is missed, you know when the next one is likely to form. Most
neophyte or novice traders lack this luxury, constantly reacting without knowing when or what is supposed
to happen for them to take a trade.

The fear of losing will lead you to seek systems with high accuracy, though high accuracy isn't a necessity.
This fear arises from not having a plan. If you lack a method with consistent results seen in hindsight,
backtesting, and walk-forward analysis, you won't have the wherewithal to embrace uncertainty as a trader.
The ability to do this is not for everyone. Every trader hits a wall and must determine if trading is truly for
them. For some, it isn't, and they may need to rely on others for trading or not trade at all. But if you're
going to trade, you cannot fear taking a loss.

section_title: PRACTICAL SOLUTIONS FOR FEAR OF LOSING
content:
Fearing a loss is often directly linked to over-leveraging and/or over-trading. Both elements are easily fixed:
- Lower your leverage, and you won't be so fearful about taking a loss because it won't hurt you as much,
even with a losing streak.
- Slow your frequency down in terms of over-trading. Even if you're not trading a high-accuracy method or
setup, high frequency won't quickly erode your equity. These are simple solutions to a common problem
among traders.

Impatience between setups is common, especially in a trader's infancy. Even with a profitable setup, good
organization, and a well-groomed money management strategy, impatience can lead to waiting for the
setup or trying to get in just before you're supposed to. For example, if you're waiting for a 50 level to buy,
but it's flirting with 65, and you can't stand it, you jump in. This needs to be dealt with over time by forging
discipline and following the rules in your method. The problem is a lack of experience to understand what
this will require of you.

section_title: THE IMPULSE TO RE-ENTER AND FORCING THE MARKET
content:
Previous exposure, preferably by demo, or eventually live trading, can lead to a 'rush' from successful
decisions. You want to feel that rush again immediately after closing a profitable trade. This impulse makes
you want to go right back in, jumping in before your setup and taking trades that aren't necessarily your
setups. As a commodity trader in the early 90s, the impulse to win again led to taking trades that were not
part of the defined setup, just to 'roll the dice' and feel good. This impatience comes from infancy and lack
of experience.

Experience will teach you one of two things:
- Losing 'sucks,' and it's better not to lose too much when you do lose.
- You're not perfect, so you have to wait for the best scenarios for your 'luck' to be in favor. We don't know if
our setup will be accurate, nor if our setups will come when expected. Forcing it or jumping ahead,
anticipating the setup, is wrong. Trading is a statistical guess with a measure of uncertainty. Waiting can
feel like water torture, especially if you're sitting in front of the charts, wanting to get into a trade and accept
wider risk. That's incorrect. Demand your price, demand your setup. By doing so, you'll combat the
impatience that novice traders always feel between setups.

section_title: DEALING WITH THE FEAR OF NOT BEING GOOD ENOUGH
content:
The fear of not being good enough is a byproduct of being on social media. Everyone seems to be better
than the next person, or trained by a 'leader,' or a 'rock star' themselves. If you're not equipped to handle
the tomfoolery on social media like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram, it's probably better not to be on it. If
you quickly feel insufficient or your learning speed isn't up to snuff compared to others online, remember
that many times, what you see online is a lie. People claim to have it all figured out when they're faking it
until they make it.

section_title: FOCUSING ON YOUR PERSONAL TRADING REPORT CARD
content:
Don't let social media comparisons wear on you or make you feel insignificant in your development. Your
report card on your own results, as detailed in your journal, is the only opinion that matters. It's not my
opinion or anyone else's. It's about whether you're seeing development and progress, measured by your
journal and, obviously, your bottom line. That's the one that truly makes the case for your development. So,
fear of not being good enough is simply a trap between your ears. Don't engage in arm-wrestling matches
about who's better or what you 'should' be making; it's a proverbial measuring contest. It's about surviving
long enough to appreciate and develop as a trader.

section_title: MANAGING FEAR OF LOSING STREAKS AND DRAWDOWN
content:
The fear of losing streaks and drawdown is directly related to money management. If you're afraid of taking
losses in any set or series, or fear drawdown, it's because you have no process or protocol in place for
what you would do should a loss or losing streak develop. A teaching was provided to help 'flatline your
losses,' which removes the effects of drawdown in your losing trades. Implementing something as simple
as that quickly changes your perspective on fear and anxiety about taking losses or a single string of
losses.

section_title: CULTIVATING DISCIPLINE AND ADHERING TO RULES
content:
Lack of discipline in following rules is human nature. If a sign says 'don't walk on the grass,' your first
impulse is often to ignore it. Like children, we do things we know we shouldn't. With a trading account
(money or demo), we do the same. We know we shouldn't do something, but because there's an
opportunity and no one is physically stopping us, we do it. The little voice in your head says 'I should not be
doing this' but you ignore it to see what happens. If you want to experiment, do it elsewhere, not in the
marketplace.

section_title: DEVELOPING LONGEVITY WITH RIGID, CONCISE RULES
content:
You must have discipline and rules, and once those rules are made, you must stick to them; do not deviate.
Over time, this forges discipline and will serve you well, especially during periods of drawdown and losing
trades. If you lack the discipline to stick to the very things that will eventually lead to longevity in this
business, you won't fare well. Discipline comes by forcing yourself to follow a set of rules. Those rules have
to be:
- Rigid
- Concise
- Detailed
- Not ambiguous
They must be very binary, meaning you do this, or you do that, or you do nothing at all. There should be
three conditions for every decision-making process: it's a go, it's a no, or you sit still and do nothing.

section_title: AVOIDING THE TRAP OF 'THE NEXT BEST THING'
content:
Searching for the 'next best thing' is one of the biggest problems. Just look at Twitter: whoever is hot right
now, whoever has the most attention, everyone flocks to them. This has always been the case, not just in
trading. Whoever 'barks loudest' draws the biggest crowd, regardless of whether they have anything
worthwhile to say. You see people who are in love with one concept or guru this month change teams and
go somewhere else next month, thinking it's a better way or an amplification of something.

section_title: FINDING COMPLETENESS IN PRICE ACTION AND TIME-BASED PRECISION
content:
It has been learned that there's nothing really better for me as a trader, so I don't look anymore. Everything
needed is known in terms of price action; the open, high, low, and close tell me everything. There isn't
anything out there that entices me personally. As the Bible says, 'there's nothing new under the sun.' If you
have arrived at something that makes sense, even if it's not perfect (perfection isn't needed), and you know
you have a statistical edge, and the things you do are:
- Highly accurate
- Very precise
- Time-based
Then you know when your setup is going to form. If you were to ask the majority of individuals trading any
asset class if they could set a time on when their next setup would occur, over 90% would say no. That's
why trading is uncertain for them, but not when you know what time certain things should happen and what
days of the week certain things should happen.
